steps.title: Identify the causal relationship in the passage
steps.content: The passage explicitly connects Barragán's travels and international exposure to a change in his work. After his trips to the United States and Europe, he “began incorporating principles derived from functionalism and modernism,” shown by comparing the Chávez Peón de Ochoa House (unadorned geometric forms) with the Aguila House (historically inspired). The main idea is this shift in aesthetic style, directly caused by his time abroad.

steps.title: Find the choice that captures the shift and its cause
steps.content: Choice B directly names what the passage demonstrates: a shift reflecting international influence. Choices A, C, and D introduce mischaracterizations the passage doesn't support—either treating both houses as modernist, reversing the timeline, or fabricating an experimentalism claim. That matches choice B — B is correct.
